IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper addresses a practical problem—improving word embeddings by incorporating ngrams into widely used methods (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD)—and demonstrates measurable gains on standard similarity and analogy benchmarks using the same datasets and baselines as Levy et al. (2015), which increases credibility. Its memory-efficient co-occurrence construction and released code improve reproducibility and make the idea easy to adopt in practice, but the gains are incremental, come with clear computational trade-offs, and some methods (SVD) show mixed results. Thus the work is useful and likely to be built upon by practitioners exploring ngram-aware embeddings, but it is not transformative for the field. | B(3): This paper addresses a practical and relevant problem—improving word representations by incorporating ngrams across several established methods—and provides a memory-efficient construction that lowers the barrier to use, which practitioners will appreciate. Demonstrated gains on standard similarity and analogy benchmarks and released code make it likely to be adopted by researchers comparing embedding variants. However, the idea of treating subword/ngram information as tokens is incremental relative to existing subword approaches (e.g., FastText/char-ngram models), results are mixed for some factorization methods, and added compute/memory costs limit transformative potential. Overall it is a useful, moderately impactful contribution that clarifies when ngram enhancements help and how to build them more efficiently.

Thus the work is useful and likely to be built upon by practitioners exploring ngram-aware embeddings, but it is not transformative for the field.

Overall it is a useful, moderately impactful contribution that clarifies when ngram enhancements help and how to build them more efficiently.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This work sits squarely in the venue’s core area: improving distributional word representations by incorporating ngrams and proposing a memory-efficient co-occurrence construction. The authors compare to canonical baselines (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD), use standard datasets (Levy et al. setup, Google/MSR analogies), report hyperparameters, and release code — all aligning with expectations for top NLP/ML conferences. From a reproducibility-focused perspective the experimental protocol and dataset/baseline choices are appropriate for the community, with stated limitations and no obvious mismatch to the venue’s audience. | B(5): This paper is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML conferences: it proposes a concrete modification to core distributional representation methods (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD) by incorporating ngrams and introduces a memory-efficient co-occurrence construction. The work evaluates on standard benchmarks (multiple similarity and analogy datasets, including Google/MSR) and compares to appropriate baselines, with code and hyperparameters reported—meeting the community’s methodological and reproducibility expectations. Its contributions (practical ngram integration across several embedding frameworks and scalability improvements) address an audience interested in representation learning and have clear relevance to ongoing research on subword/ngram models and matrix-factorization approaches.

reviewer_a.justification: This work sits squarely in the venue’s core area: improving distributional word representations by incorporating ngrams and proposing a memory-efficient co-occurrence construction. The authors compare to canonical baselines (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD), use standard datasets (Levy et al. setup, Google/MSR analogies), report hyperparameters, and release code — all aligning with expectations for top NLP/ML conferences. From a reproducibility-focused perspective the experimental protocol and dataset/baseline choices are appropriate for the community, with stated limitations and no obvious mismatch to the venue’s audience.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML conferences: it proposes a concrete modification to core distributional representation methods (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD) by incorporating ngrams and introduces a memory-efficient co-occurrence construction. The work evaluates on standard benchmarks (multiple similarity and analogy datasets, including Google/MSR) and compares to appropriate baselines, with code and hyperparameters reported—meeting the community’s methodological and reproducibility expectations. Its contributions (practical ngram integration across several embedding frameworks and scalability improvements) address an audience interested in representation learning and have clear relevance to ongoing research on subword/ngram models and matrix-factorization approaches.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear progression from motivation through method to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions (introducing ngrams into four embedding methods and a memory-efficient co-occurrence construction) are stated early. Notation and hyperparameter reporting are described as consistent and present, and figures/tables/examples are reported as effective and informative. Writing quality is noted as good with adequate related-work coverage, though a few minor presentation refinements could make it an exceptional read for reproducibility-focused readers like myself. | B(4): The paper is well-organized and follows a clear structure (introduction → method → experiments → conclusion), with the main contributions stated early and code/hyperparameters reported for reproducibility. Notation and methodology are presented consistently, and related work coverage is adequate for situating the approach among SGNS/GloVe/PPMI/SVD baselines. Figures/tables and examples appear informative for the experiments on multiple similarity and analogy datasets, and overall writing quality is good, though some sections (e.g., interaction of ngrams with SVD hyperparameters) could benefit from slightly more exposition and clearer discussion of limitations.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper appears well-organized with a clear progression from motivation through method to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions (introducing ngrams into four embedding methods and a memory-efficient co-occurrence construction) are stated early. Notation and hyperparameter reporting are described as consistent and present, and figures/tables/examples are reported as effective and informative. Writing quality is noted as good with adequate related-work coverage, though a few minor presentation refinements could make it an exceptional read for reproducibility-focused readers like myself.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well-organized and follows a clear structure (introduction → method → experiments → conclusion), with the main contributions stated early and code/hyperparameters reported for reproducibility. Notation and methodology are presented consistently, and related work coverage is adequate for situating the approach among SGNS/GloVe/PPMI/SVD baselines. Figures/tables and examples appear informative for the experiments on multiple similarity and analogy datasets, and overall writing quality is good, though some sections (e.g., interaction of ngrams with SVD hyperparameters) could benefit from slightly more exposition and clearer discussion of limitations.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates ngram-augmented SGNS, GloVe, PPMI and SVD on multiple standard benchmarks (six similarity datasets and two analogy sets including Google/MSR) and compares to appropriate baselines, releases code, and reports hyperparameters — supporting the main claims. It also discusses and measures some negative results (e.g., ngram–ngram co-occurrence costs and SVD bi-gram degradations), which serves as a useful ablation. However, important reproducibility/sensitivity analyses are missing: no reporting of run variance or statistical significance, limited exploration/tuning of hyperparameters specific to ngram inclusion, and only partial ablations isolating ngram effects versus other implementation differences. These gaps prevent a top score despite generally solid experimental breadth. | B(4): The paper provides solid empirical evidence across standard similarity and analogy benchmarks, compares to appropriate baselines (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD), reports hyperparameters and releases code, and explicitly discusses several negative results (ngram-ngram costs, degraded SVD performance). It lacks some deeper sensitivity analyses and targeted ablations (e.g., systematic tuning of hyperparameters for ngram inclusion, more downstream task evaluations beyond intrinsic tests) and could better situate itself against closely related subword methods like Bojanowski et al. (FastText). Overall the experiments are thorough enough to support the central claims but leave room for additional analysis.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates ngram-augmented SGNS, GloVe, PPMI and SVD on multiple standard benchmarks (six similarity datasets and two analogy sets including Google/MSR) and compares to appropriate baselines, releases code, and reports hyperparameters — supporting the main claims. It also discusses and measures some negative results (e.g., ngram–ngram co-occurrence costs and SVD bi-gram degradations), which serves as a useful ablation. However, important reproducibility/sensitivity analyses are missing: no reporting of run variance or statistical significance, limited exploration/tuning of hyperparameters specific to ngram inclusion, and only partial ablations isolating ngram effects versus other implementation differences. These gaps prevent a top score despite generally solid experimental breadth.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides solid empirical evidence across standard similarity and analogy benchmarks, compares to appropriate baselines (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD), reports hyperparameters and releases code, and explicitly discusses several negative results (ngram-ngram costs, degraded SVD performance). It lacks some deeper sensitivity analyses and targeted ablations (e.g., systematic tuning of hyperparameters for ngram inclusion, more downstream task evaluations beyond intrinsic tests) and could better situate itself against closely related subword methods like Bojanowski et al. (FastText). Overall the experiments are thorough enough to support the central claims but leave room for additional analysis.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper states clear empirical claims and evaluates them against appropriate baselines (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD) on standard similarity and analogy datasets, and provides code and hyperparameter settings — supporting reproducibility in principle. However, from the summary I see missing rigor in experimental controls: there is no mention of variance across runs or statistical significance testing, and the authors explicitly did not retune hyperparameters for the ngram-augmented models which could confound the reported gains. Important ablations (e.g., isolating the contribution of adding ngrams vs other implementation details) and more detailed cost/complexity measurements are not clearly reported. These gaps raise concerns about whether the improvements are robust, so while the methodology is promising I cannot fully endorse all claims as conclusively supported. | B(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments across multiple embedding algorithms (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD) and standard similarity/analogy benchmarks, and the authors provide an implemented, memory-efficient co-occurrence construction and open code. Mathematical aspects (use of negative sampling to approximate conditional probabilities and treating ngrams as tokens) are standard and appear correctly applied; the SVD/PPMI treatment aligns with prior work. Methodologically, they compare against appropriate baselines and follow established hyper-parameters, but not tuning hyper-parameters for ngram models and lacking reported statistical significance tests are minor gaps that temper confidence in some gains. Overall the results and stated limitations support the conclusions, with only modest methodological caveats.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear empirical claims and evaluates them against appropriate baselines (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD) on standard similarity and analogy datasets, and provides code and hyperparameter settings — supporting reproducibility in principle. However, from the summary I see missing rigor in experimental controls: there is no mention of variance across runs or statistical significance testing, and the authors explicitly did not retune hyperparameters for the ngram-augmented models which could confound the reported gains. Important ablations (e.g., isolating the contribution of adding ngrams vs other implementation details) and more detailed cost/complexity measurements are not clearly reported. These gaps raise concerns about whether the improvements are robust, so while the methodology is promising I cannot fully endorse all claims as conclusively supported.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments across multiple embedding algorithms (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD) and standard similarity/analogy benchmarks, and the authors provide an implemented, memory-efficient co-occurrence construction and open code. Mathematical aspects (use of negative sampling to approximate conditional probabilities and treating ngrams as tokens) are standard and appear correctly applied; the SVD/PPMI treatment aligns with prior work. Methodologically, they compare against appropriate baselines and follow established hyper-parameters, but not tuning hyper-parameters for ngram models and lacking reported statistical significance tests are minor gaps that temper confidence in some gains. Overall the results and stated limitations support the conclusions, with only modest methodological caveats.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper offers a straightforward, pragmatic extension—treating ngrams as tokens and adding them to SGNS, GloVe, PPMI and SVD—which is useful but conceptually incremental over prior subword/ngram work (e.g., “Enriching Word Vectors with Subword Information”). Its memory-efficient co-occurrence matrix construction and empirical observations (e.g., limited gain from ngram–ngram co-occurrences, measured gains on Google analogies) are the clearest novel elements, but the core idea is a non‑trivial engineering refinement rather than a fundamentally new modeling paradigm. Given the close relation to existing subword/ngram approaches and the paper’s focus on integrating those into standard methods, I rate the novelty as moderate. | B(3): The paper offers a coherent engineering of including ngrams as ordinary context tokens across multiple embedding frameworks (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD) and proposes a memory-efficient way to build ngram co-occurrence matrices—this is a non-trivial, practically useful extension. However, conceptually it overlaps substantially with prior subword/ngram approaches (e.g., Bojanowski et al. 2017 "Enriching Word Vectors with Subword Information") and an existing Ngram2vec line is already noted, so the core idea is incremental rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. The empirical observations (e.g., limited benefit of ngram–ngram counts and sizable gains from bigrams on Google analogies) are informative to practitioners but do not by themselves constitute high conceptual novelty.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper offers a straightforward, pragmatic extension—treating ngrams as tokens and adding them to SGNS, GloVe, PPMI and SVD—which is useful but conceptually incremental over prior subword/ngram work (e.g., “Enriching Word Vectors with Subword Information”). Its memory-efficient co-occurrence matrix construction and empirical observations (e.g., limited gain from ngram–ngram co-occurrences, measured gains on Google analogies) are the clearest novel elements, but the core idea is a non‑trivial engineering refinement rather than a fundamentally new modeling paradigm. Given the close relation to existing subword/ngram approaches and the paper’s focus on integrating those into standard methods, I rate the novelty as moderate.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper offers a coherent engineering of including ngrams as ordinary context tokens across multiple embedding frameworks (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD) and proposes a memory-efficient way to build ngram co-occurrence matrices—this is a non-trivial, practically useful extension. However, conceptually it overlaps substantially with prior subword/ngram approaches (e.g., Bojanowski et al. 2017 "Enriching Word Vectors with Subword Information") and an existing Ngram2vec line is already noted, so the core idea is incremental rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. The empirical observations (e.g., limited benefit of ngram–ngram counts and sizable gains from bigrams on Google analogies) are informative to practitioners but do not by themselves constitute high conceptual novelty.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against the standard and appropriate baselines (SGNS/word2vec, GloVe, PPMI and SVD) using the same datasets and evaluation metrics as Levy et al. (2015), reports hyperparameters and provides code, which supports fair comparisons. The authors discuss cases where n-gram inclusion does not help (e.g., ngram–ngram co-occurrence provides little gain and SVD bi-grams sometimes hurt) and acknowledge computational costs and limitations. One notable gap is lack of direct empirical comparison to contemporary subword/ngram-based methods (e.g., Bojanowski et al.'s subword enrichment / fastText-style approaches), which would be the most relevant competing family; otherwise the experimental design and reporting are sound. | B(4): The paper evaluates against the core and recent baselines in embedding literature (SGNS/word2vec, GloVe, PPMI and SVD) on standard similarity and analogy benchmarks, and uses the same datasets and metrics as prior work (Levy et al.), which supports fair comparisons. The authors are explicit about hyperparameter choices (following baselines) and acknowledge cases where SVD with bigrams underperforms, but they note they did not retune hyperparameters for ngrams—this is a minor fairness concern. Coverage of related work is reported as adequate, though direct empirical comparison to strong subword/ngram approaches (e.g., Bojanowski et al.'s subword model / FastText-style methods) appears absent; overall the comparisons are solid with only small omissions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against the standard and appropriate baselines (SGNS/word2vec, GloVe, PPMI and SVD) using the same datasets and evaluation metrics as Levy et al. (2015), reports hyperparameters and provides code, which supports fair comparisons. The authors discuss cases where n-gram inclusion does not help (e.g., ngram–ngram co-occurrence provides little gain and SVD bi-grams sometimes hurt) and acknowledge computational costs and limitations. One notable gap is lack of direct empirical comparison to contemporary subword/ngram-based methods (e.g., Bojanowski et al.'s subword enrichment / fastText-style approaches), which would be the most relevant competing family; otherwise the experimental design and reporting are sound.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates against the core and recent baselines in embedding literature (SGNS/word2vec, GloVe, PPMI and SVD) on standard similarity and analogy benchmarks, and uses the same datasets and metrics as prior work (Levy et al.), which supports fair comparisons. The authors are explicit about hyperparameter choices (following baselines) and acknowledge cases where SVD with bigrams underperforms, but they note they did not retune hyperparameters for ngrams—this is a minor fairness concern. Coverage of related work is reported as adequate, though direct empirical comparison to strong subword/ngram approaches (e.g., Bojanowski et al.'s subword model / FastText-style methods) appears absent; overall the comparisons are solid with only small omissions.

main_claims: Introduce ngrams into four representation methods (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI matrix and its SVD factorization) to improve word representations.
Ngram-enhanced models learn improved word embeddings as demonstrated on word similarity and analogy tasks.
Trained ngram embeddings reflect semantic meanings and syntactic patterns (useful for tasks like finding antonyms and collocations).
Propose a novel, more memory-efficient way of building ngram co-occurrence matrices to reduce hardware requirements.
_summary.methodology: Treat ngrams as normal tokens and include them in the context vocabulary for SGNS, GloVe, PPMI and SVD (e.g., center words predict surrounding ngrams), and build ngram co-occurrence matrices with a novel memory-efficient construction; SGNS uses negative sampling to approximate conditional probabilities.
datasets: Same datasets as Levy et al. (2015): six similarity datasets and two analogy datasets
metrics: Word analogy (additive and multiplicative evaluation functions)
limitations: Including ngrams increases computational and memory costs (ngram-ngram co-occurrence brings little improvement but immense costs).
In SVD, adding bi-grams sometimes leads to worse results for analogy and similarity tasks.
The authors strictly followed baseline hyper-parameters and did not tune them for ngrams; relationships between ngrams and hyperparameters require further exploration.

reviewer_a.name: Reproducibility-Focused Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with 10+ years building and evaluating NLP models in industry and academia; frequent contributor to shared tasks and reproducibility workshops.
traits: insists on thorough baselines and consistent experimental conditions (same vocab, corpus, preprocessing, dimensionality, and training steps)
looks for ablation studies that isolate the contribution of ngram inclusion vs other implementation details
values statistical rigor: significance testing, variance across runs, and clear hyperparameter reporting
checks implementation practicality: memory/time complexity claims, reproducible code release, and dataset / seed availability
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Evaluates papers by inspecting experimental design and reproducibility: I judge whether the empirical claims are convincingly supported by well-controlled, repeatable experiments and clear implementation details.

reviewer_b.name: Literature- and Impact-Oriented NLP Researcher
reviewer_b.background: Professor-level NLP researcher focused on distributional semantics and representation learning; long track record of work on word embeddings, compositionality, and linguistic evaluation.
traits: assesses novelty and relation to prior work on subword/ngram models (e.g., FastText, char/ngram embeddings, phrase embeddings) and matrix factorization approaches
examines broader significance: when and why ngram-enhanced representations matter for downstream tasks and linguistic phenomena
looks for qualitative analysis that links learned embeddings to interpretable linguistic patterns and potential applications
evaluates scalability and real-world adoption potential, including trade-offs versus existing approaches
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Judges a paper primarily on its novelty, placement in the literature, and practical significance, emphasizing conceptual contributions and whether the work advances understanding or practice in the field.
